nameMeanings.name: Edward
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English and Welsh: from the Middle English personal name Edward (Old English Ēadweard, from ēad ‘prosperity, fortune’ + w(e)ard ‘guard, ward’). The popularity of the personal name was largely due to its being the name of the sainted Anglo-Saxon king Edward the Confessor and of three kings in the 13th–14th centuries.

variants.name: Bedward
meanings: Welsh (Staffordshire and Welsh Marches): from the patronymic ap Edward ‘son of Edward’ (see Edward ).

variants.name: Ewart
meanings: English:
from the Middle English (Old French) personal name Ewart, a French form of Edward .

occupational name from Middle English ewehird ‘ewe herd, keeper of the sheep’ (Old English ēowu + hierde). Ewes were kept for their milk, from which cheese was made. The name may also have survived as Howard .

variants.name: Eduardo
meanings: Portuguese and Spanish: from the personal name Eduardo (see Edward ).

variants.name: Edwards
meanings: English and Welsh: variant of Edward , with genitival or post-medieval excrescent -s. This surname is also very common among African Americans.

History: One of the earliest American bearers of this very common English surname was William Edwards, the son of Rev. Richard Edwards, a London clergyman in the age of Elizabeth I, who came to New England c. 1640. His descendant Jonathan (1703–58), of East Windsor, CT, was a prominent Congregational clergyman whose New England theology led to the first Great Awakening, a great religious revival.

variants.name: Edwardsen
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Scandinavian Egil.  German Fritz, Hans.
Americanized form (and a rare Danish and Norwegian variant) of Danish and Norwegian Edvardsen: patronymic from the personal name Edvard, Scandinavian form of Edward .

variants.name: Duarte
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Manuel, Carlos, Jesus, Juan, Jorge, Luis, Ruben, Francisco, Miguel, Raul, Pedro.  Portuguese Joao, Joaquim, Agostinho, Henrique, Paulo, Albano, Almir, Anabela, Ilidio, Ligia, Marcio, Margarida.
Portuguese: from the personal name Duarte, a Portuguese name of uncertain origin taken to be the equivalent of Edward .

variants.name: Edouard
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French/Haitian Pierre, Andre, Alain, Alphonse, Auguste, Carolle, Chantel, Damien, Edouard, Ermite, Eunide, Gabrielle.
French, West Indian (mainly Haiti), and Mauritian (Édouard): from the French personal name Édouard, an equivalent of Edward . This surname is most common in Haiti.

variants.name: Edwardson
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Scandinavian Hokan, Nels.
English (Lancashire): patronymic from the personal name Edward + son.
Americanized form (and a rare Swedish variant) of Swedish Edvardsson: patronymic from the personal name Edvard, Scandinavian form of Edward .

nameMeanings.name: Llywelyn
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: (Welsh)  See  Llewelyn .

variants.name: Llewelyn
meanings: Traditional: altered form (influenced by llew ‘lion’) of Llywelyn, an ancient name of uncertain derivation. It probably goes back to the Old Celtic name Lugobelinos, the first element of which is Lugu- (the name of a god; see Lleu ); the second is a name-forming element found also in names such as Cunobelinus (Cymbeline). The name was borne by Llywelyn ap Iorwerth ( 1173–1240 ) and his grandson Llywelyn ap Gruffydd ( d. 1282 ), Welsh princes who for a time united their countrymen in North Wales and led opposition to the power of the Norman barons in South Wales and the Marches.

nameMeanings.name: ap
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of any of the names beginning with this syllable.

variants.name: Alexander
meanings: From the Latin form of the Greek name Alexandros, from alexein ‘to defend’ + anēr ‘man, warrior’ (genitive andros). The name became extremely popular in the post-classical period, and was borne by several individuals in the New Testament and some early Christian saints. Its use as a common given name throughout Europe, however, derives largely from the fame of Alexander the Great , King of Macedon ( 356–323 bc ), around whom a large body of popular legend grew up in late antiquity, much of which came to be embodied in the medieval ‘Alexander romances’.

variants.name: Alan
meanings: Of Celtic origin and uncertain derivation (possibly a diminutive of a word meaning ‘rock’). It was introduced into England by Breton followers of William the Conqueror , most notably Alan , Earl of Brittany, who was rewarded for his services with vast estates in the newly conquered kingdom. In Britain the variants Allan and Allen are considerably less frequent, and generally represent transferred uses of surname forms, whereas in America all three forms of the name are approximately equally common. See also Alun .

variants.name: Albert
meanings: From an Old French name, Albert, of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from adal ‘noble’ + berht ‘bright, famous’. This was adopted by the Normans and introduced by them to England, displacing the Old English form Æþelbeorht. The name is popular in a variety of forms in Western Europe, and has been traditional in a number of European princely families. It was out of favour in England for centuries, however, and the revival of its popularity in the 19th century was largely in honour of Queen Victoria's consort, Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg-Gotha .

en.lifeSummary: Llywelyn ap Edward was born in 1460, in Henllan, Denbighshire, Wales, United Kingdom. He had at least 3 sons with Margred ferch Roger.
